385.question: When would you use Thread vs Runnable vs Callable in real code?
385.answer: Thread, Runnable, and Callable serve different purposes in Java's concurrency model. Thread is a class you extend for simple sequential work, but inheritance limits you since Java doesn't support multiple inheritance. Runnable is preferred for tasks—implement Runnable and pass to Thread or ExecutorService. Runnable.run() returns void and can't throw checked exceptions, making it unsuitable for operations that need error handling or results. Callable<T> is used when you need a return value or exception throwing. In production, ExecutorService accepts Callables and returns Future<T>, enabling non-blocking result retrieval and timeouts. For simple background work, use Runnable with ExecutorService. For operations requiring results or proper exception handling (DB queries, API calls), use Callable. Avoid extending Thread directly in professional code—it couples task logic to threading.
385.diagram: Thread (legacy) → Runnable (void, no result) → Callable<T> (returns T, checked exceptions)

386.question: What does volatile actually guarantee, and why it doesn't make code thread-safe?
386.answer: volatile guarantees visibility—writes are immediately visible to other threads—but provides no atomicity or mutual exclusion. When Thread A writes to a volatile field, Thread B sees that exact value immediately (no CPU cache delays). However, volatile cannot protect compound operations (read-modify-write). volatile x = 5; then x++ is NOT atomic—another thread can read x between the read and increment. For thread-safety, you need synchronized blocks, locks, or atomic variables. volatile works only for simple flag updates (shutdown signals) or publishing immutable objects. The Java Memory Model guarantees volatile fields establish happens-before relationships: writes to volatile variables happen-before reads by another thread.
386.diagram: volatile: [Guarantees visibility, ordering] → [No atomicity, no mutual exclusion] → Use for flags, not operations

387.question: How the Java Memory Model impacts visibility and ordering
387.answer: Java Memory Model (JMM) defines how threads interact with memory. Without JMM, compiler optimizations and CPU reordering would make multithreading unpredictable. JMM establishes happens-before relationships: if Action A happens-before Action B, A's effects are visible to B. Key rules: (1) Thread start happens-before first action in thread, (2) volatile write happens-before subsequent volatile read, (3) synchronized release happens-before synchronized acquire, (4) all actions in one thread happen-before thread.join() returns. Without happens-before, writes cached in CPU registers never reach main memory, causing other threads to see stale data. Proper synchronization (synchronized, volatile, locks) creates happens-before edges, forcing visibility. In production, incorrect ordering causes race conditions visible only under load.

388.question: Why sleep() and wait() solve very different problems
388.answer: sleep() pauses current thread for fixed duration without releasing locks, while wait() releases the monitor lock and resumes when notified. sleep() is for delays (pausing execution), wait() is for coordination between threads. If you sleep(1000) in synchronized block, the lock is held for entire 1 second, blocking other threads from entering. If you wait(1000), the lock is released immediately, allowing other threads to acquire it. wait() requires synchronized context and locks.wait() throws InterruptedException. Use sleep() for timeout-based retries or periodic checks. Use wait() with notify()/notifyAll() for producer-consumer coordination. In modern code, avoid both—use CountDownLatch, CyclicBarrier, or CompletableFuture instead.
388.diagram: sleep(ms) → [Holds Lock, Fixed Pause] vs wait() → [Releases Lock, Until notify()]

389.question: synchronized vs ReentrantLock and why both still matter
389.answer: synchronized is built-in, implicit, and managed by JVM—automatically releases even if exception occurs. ReentrantLock is explicit, allowing try-finally release, with features like fairness, interruption, and condition variables. synchronized is easier but less flexible. ReentrantLock allows fair queuing (threads acquire in FIFO order), interruption (lock.lockInterruptibly()), and timeouts (lock.tryLock(time)). synchronized has no timeout or interruption support. ReentrantLock's conditions enable multiple wait sets (different Condition objects for different producer/consumer groups). However, synchronized performs better in low-contention scenarios due to JVM optimizations (biased locking, lock coarsening). In high-contention scenarios, ReentrantLock's fairness prevents starvation. Both still matter: synchronized for simple critical sections, ReentrantLock for complex coordination.
389.diagram: synchronized (simple, implicit) ←→ ReentrantLock (flexible, explicit, fair, conditions)

390.question: Intrinsic locks vs explicit locks and when fairness is important
390.answer: Intrinsic locks (synchronized) use object monitors—every Java object has a monitor. JVM automatically acquires/releases based on method/block scope. Explicit locks (ReentrantLock, ReadWriteLock) are dedicated objects for locking. Intrinsic locks are always non-fair by default—no guaranteed FIFO ordering. Under contention, any waiting thread can acquire, causing starvation if a thread repeatedly tries to acquire. Fair explicit locks use FIFO queues—threads acquire in the order they requested. Fairness matters when: (1) some threads need predictable latency (not starved indefinitely), (2) preventing writer starvation in ReadWriteLock (many readers block one writer). Cost: fair locks are slower due to queue management. In low-contention scenarios, both are similar. In high-traffic payment systems where latency is critical, fairness prevents some requests being perpetually delayed.
390.diagram: Intrinsic Locks (non-fair, starvation risk) vs Explicit Fair Locks (FIFO, predictable latency)

391.question: How deadlocks happen in production, not just in theory
391.answer: Deadlocks require: (1) mutual exclusion (locks held), (2) hold-and-wait (thread holds lock and waits for another), (3) no preemption (can't force lock release), (4) circular wait (T1 waits for T2's lock, T2 waits for T1's lock). In production, deadlock detection is hard—the system silently hangs. Common scenario: Thread 1 acquires Account A's lock, waits for Account B. Thread 2 acquires Account B, waits for Account A. Opposite lock ordering causes deadlock. Another: callback from locked resource (DB calls back into application code that tries to re-acquire). Solution: always acquire locks in consistent order (sort account IDs before locking), use timeouts (tryLock with duration), or avoid nested locks. Detecting production deadlock requires jstack or JVM thread dump showing BLOCKED threads in circular dependencies.
391.diagram: Circular Wait (T1→T2→T1) + Mutex + Hold & Wait = DEADLOCK (system hangs, no error thrown)

392.question: Choosing between fixed, cached, and work-stealing thread pools
392.answer: FixedThreadPool(n): Creates n threads, queues overflow tasks. Use for consistent throughput (API requests with known peak). If task queue fills up, new tasks are rejected or block (depending on RejectedExecutionHandler). CachedThreadPool: Creates new threads as needed, reuses idle threads (60s timeout). Use for bursty workloads (many short tasks with gaps). Risk: unbounded thread creation under sustained load crashes JVM. WorkStealingPool (ForkJoinPool): Uses work-stealing—idle threads steal tasks from busy threads' queues. Optimal for CPU-bound parallel tasks (fork-join decomposition). Has overhead, unsuitable for I/O-bound tasks. For API server: FixedThreadPool. For background jobs: CachedThreadPool. For data processing: WorkStealingPool. Unbounded queues can cause OutOfMemory—use bounded queues with explicit rejection policy.
392.diagram: FixedThreadPool (bounded threads) → CachedThreadPool (risky unbounded) → WorkStealingPool (CPU-bound only)

393.question: Sizing thread pools for CPU-bound vs IO-bound workloads
393.answer: For CPU-bound (compute-heavy): Size = number of cores. More threads cause context switching overhead. One thread per core minimizes switching. For I/O-bound (network, DB): Size = cores * 2 to cores * 10 (depends on I/O latency). While one thread waits for I/O, another runs. Formula: threads = cores * (1 + wait_time / compute_time). If request takes 100ms total, 90ms waiting for DB: threads = cores * (1 + 0.9/0.1) = cores * 10. Measure empirically: start with cores * 2, load test, monitor CPU utilization and response time. CPU at 80-90% is ideal. If CPU <50%, increase threads. If response time increases, you're over-threaded (too many threads chasing limited resources). Common mistake: CachedThreadPool for sustained I/O creates unbounded threads, exhausting system memory.
393.diagram: CPU-bound: [cores threads, minimal context switching] vs I/O-bound: [cores * (2-10), threads waiting on I/O]

394.question: What really happens when executor queues fill up
394.answer: When ThreadPoolExecutor queue is full (capacity reached), the RejectedExecutionHandler decides behavior: (1) AbortPolicy (default): throws RejectedExecutionException—calling thread gets exception, (2) CallerRunsPolicy: executes task in caller's thread, blocking it, (3) DiscardPolicy: silently drops task, (4) DiscardOldestPolicy: removes oldest task, adds new one. In production, AbortPolicy is dangerous—exception crashes the endpoint. CallerRunsPolicy causes backpressure—if queue fills, client threads slow down, naturally limiting load. DiscardPolicy loses requests silently (bad). Custom handlers can log metrics or reroute tasks. Bounded queues with proper rejection policy are essential for stability.
394.diagram: Queue Full → [AbortPolicy: Exception] / [CallerRunsPolicy: Block Caller] / [DiscardPolicy: Silent Loss]

395.question: When CompletableFuture improves clarity and when it hides complexity
395.answer: CompletableFuture chains asynchronous operations, enabling non-blocking composition (thenApply, thenCompose). Improves clarity when: (1) chaining independent async operations, (2) timeout handling, (3) error recovery with handle/exceptionally. Hides complexity when: (1) multiple levels of nesting (callback hell—thenCompose(x -> thenCompose(y -> ...)), (2) exception handling across stages (exceptions swallowed silently if not handled), (3) shared mutable state (concurrent modifications to captured variables), (4) thread pool context (each stage can run in different thread, causing context loss). In simple cases (call API, transform result, return), CompletableFuture is cleaner than callbacks. In complex flows with multiple error paths and dependencies, reactive frameworks (Reactor, RxJava) or virtual threads (Java 21) provide better readability.
395.diagram: CompletableFuture: [Simple chains: clear] → [Deep nesting: callback hell] → [Use reactive libs for complexity]

396.question: Why ConcurrentHashMap scales better than synchronized collections
396.answer: synchronized collections (Collections.synchronizedMap) synchronize entire map on every operation—one lock for entire structure. Under contention, all threads compete for single lock, causing serialization. ConcurrentHashMap uses segment/bucket-level locking: 16 segments by default (Java 8+), each with own lock. Multiple threads can modify different segments simultaneously (true parallelism). If Thread 1 locks segment 0 and Thread 2 locks segment 5, both operate concurrently. Contention only occurs when accessing same segment. ConcurrentHashMap is faster under high concurrency but slightly slower when single-threaded (due to hashing overhead). In Java 8+, ConcurrentHashMap uses striped locks (finer granularity). Always use ConcurrentHashMap in multi-threaded code; synchronized collections are essentially deprecated for new code.
396.diagram: synchronized (one lock, serialized) vs ConcurrentHashMap (16 locks, parallelized) = 10x throughput

397.question: What happens internally when multiple threads update the same map
397.answer: In synchronized HashMap, operation sequence: (1) compute hash, (2) navigate bucket chain, (3) find/insert entry, (4) update size. Full lock protects entire sequence. In ConcurrentHashMap: (1) hash directs to segment, (2) acquire segment lock, (3) update within segment, (4) release lock. If two threads hash to different segments, they proceed concurrently. If same segment, one waits. Concurrent put increases segment's modification counter (used by iterators). If segment exceeds load factor, rehashing happens only for that segment (not entire map). Reads in ConcurrentHashMap don't require locks (volatile reads guarantee visibility), making them very fast. Concurrent modification exception doesn't occur because iterator works on snapshot. Under hood: ConcurrentHashMap maintains volatile fields for visibility, uses CAS (Compare-And-Swap) for thread-safe operations on segment metadata.
397.diagram: Multiple threads → Hash to segments → Different segments (concurrent) or same segment (serialized) → Volatile reads (lock-free)

398.question: When CopyOnWriteArrayList is a good idea and when it's a terrible one
398.answer: CopyOnWriteArrayList creates new array copy on every write—trades write cost for read performance and thread-safety without locks. Good when: (1) reads vastly outnumber writes (e.g., event listeners, configuration), (2) write frequency is low (<1% of operations), (3) small list size. Terrible when: (1) frequent writes (every write copies entire array), (2) large lists (copying 1M elements is expensive), (3) latency-sensitive code (write pauses for copy). Example: event listener list (few adds, many reads). Counter-example: cache with frequent insertions and large size. Use when read-heavy immutable snapshots are acceptable. Memory overhead: two arrays in memory. Garbage: old array eligible for GC only after write completes and no iterators reference it. Iteration creates snapshot, concurrent writes don't affect iterator.
398.diagram: CopyOnWriteArrayList: [Read-heavy: excellent] [Write-heavy: terrible] [Small lists: good] [Large lists: bad]

399.question: Making a service thread-safe without destroying throughput
399.answer: Use fine-grained locking: lock at data structure level, not entire service. Partition data (sharding): 100 accounts distributed across 10 locks instead of 1. Lock-free algorithms (AtomicInteger, AtomicReference, ConcurrentHashMap): avoid locks entirely using CAS. Immutable objects: no synchronization needed if objects never change. Read-write locks: many readers, few writers. Thread-local storage: eliminate sharing. For payment service with 10K accounts: lock per account (not service-wide), lock 2 accounts during transfer, allow other transfers in parallel. Read-write lock on config: many reads, occasional writes. AtomicLong for counters. ConcurrentHashMap for caches. Avoid synchronized on method (coarse-grained), prefer lock (fine-grained) or atomic variables. Measure: atomic variables fastest, read-write lock faster than synchronized, synchronized acceptable at low contention.
399.diagram: Entire Service Lock (bottleneck) → Per-Data Lock (better) → Atomic Variables (best) → Lock-Free CAS (fastest)

400.question: Handling shared mutable state in high-traffic microservices
400.answer: Shared mutable state is the enemy of scalability. Strategies: (1) Eliminate sharing—replicate state per instance (stateless services), (2) Distributed locking—Redis locks for cross-instance coordination, (3) Eventual consistency—accept stale reads, reconcile asynchronously, (4) CQRS—separate read model from write model, (5) Event sourcing—immutable event log, rebuild state on demand. In microservices, typical pattern: API instances are stateless, state lives in database or cache. Database provides ACID guarantees but is bottleneck. Distributed cache (Redis) with optimistic locking (version numbers) prevents lost updates. For high-traffic systems: sharding at database level (partition by user_id), each shard owns part of state, no cross-shard locking needed. If must share (e.g., distributed counter), use eventual consistency—accept stale values or eventual reconciliation.
400.diagram: Shared State (bottleneck) → Stateless Services (scale) + Database (ACID) / Redis (cache, distributed locks)

401.question: Debugging race conditions that only appear in production
401.answer: Race conditions are timing-dependent, hard to reproduce. Local testing with few threads won't trigger issues appearing under high load. Debugging approach: (1) Enable logging with timestamps and thread IDs, (2) Use jstack periodically to capture thread states, (3) Look for inconsistent data states (e.g., balance != sum of transactions), (4) Load test locally to stress-test concurrency, (5) Add assertions (e.g., invariant checks after critical sections), (6) Use ThreadSanitizer or similar tools, (7) Review synchronization carefully—missing volatile, incorrect locking, (8) Check for double-checked locking (broken pattern), (9) Use concurrent stress test tools (jcstress). In production, high frequency of errors indicates race condition. Solution: fix synchronization, use ConcurrentHashMap instead of synchronized, atomic variables, or lock-free algorithms. Common: missing synchronized, volatile, or lock. Pattern: intermittent errors, only under load, data corruption.
401.diagram: Race Condition: [Local test: rare] → [High load: frequent] → [Fix synchronization: eliminate race]

402.question: Difference between CopyOnWriteArrayList and ConcurrentLinkedQueue
402.answer: CopyOnWriteArrayList: writes copy entire array, reads are fast, iteration is safe, best for read-heavy read-only iteration. Use for listeners, configurations. ConcurrentLinkedQueue: lock-free queue using CAS, efficient for producer-consumer, unbounded size, iteration doesn't guarantee consistent view. CopyOnWriteArrayList supports random access (get(i)), ConcurrentLinkedQueue is queue only (FIFO). CopyOnWriteArrayList wastes memory (two arrays), ConcurrentLinkedQueue is memory-efficient. For frequent adds/removes: use ConcurrentLinkedQueue. For mostly-read: use CopyOnWriteArrayList. CopyOnWriteArrayList iteration is snapshot, ConcurrentLinkedQueue iteration weakly consistent. If frequent writes, avoid CopyOnWriteArrayList entirely.
402.diagram: CopyOnWriteArrayList (snapshot iteration, read-heavy) vs ConcurrentLinkedQueue (lock-free, write-heavy, FIFO)

403.question: AtomicInteger, AtomicReference, and when to use each
403.answer: Atomic variables provide lock-free thread-safety using CAS (Compare-And-Swap). AtomicInteger: holds int, thread-safe increment/decrement/get/set. Use for counters, flags. AtomicReference: holds any object reference, useful for non-blocking updates to immutable objects. AtomicLong: holds long (better than AtomicInteger for counters due to no boxing). AtomicBoolean: holds boolean flag. Atomic arrays: thread-safe arrays. LongAdder: more efficient than AtomicLong under high contention (stripes updates across threads). Use AtomicInteger for simple counters, AtomicReference for state machines with immutable states, LongAdder for high-frequency counters. Avoid if you need complex logic (multiple fields)—use proper synchronization. Atomic variables are faster than locks in low contention, but don't scale as well as locks under high contention due to CAS retry overhead.
403.diagram: AtomicInteger (counter) → AtomicReference (state) → LongAdder (high contention counter) vs Locks (complex logic)

404.question: Compare-And-Swap (CAS) and how it enables lock-free algorithms
404.answer: CAS is a CPU instruction: compareAndSwap(memory, expectedValue, newValue) atomically checks if memory equals expectedValue, and if so, sets it to newValue, returning success/failure. All lock-free algorithms are built on CAS. Example: incrementing counter without locks: (1) read current value (5), (2) compute new value (6), (3) CAS: if current still 5, set to 6. If another thread changed it, CAS fails, retry. Unlike locks, CAS never blocks—failure means retry. Advantages: no priority inversion, deadlock-free, no context switching. Disadvantages: CAS retry loops under high contention cause spin overhead. ABA problem: if value changes from A to B back to A, CAS thinks unchanged. Solved with versioning (AtomicStampedReference). Modern CPUs have efficient CAS; JVM compiles lock-free code optimally. For high-contention scenarios, striped locks (multiple locks per operation) or lock-free data structures (ConcurrentLinkedQueue) are more efficient than spinlock retry loops.
404.diagram: CAS: [Read] → [Compute] → [Compare & Swap] (atomic) → Success or Retry (never blocks)

405.question: ThreadLocal and when to use it
405.answer: ThreadLocal provides per-thread storage—each thread gets its own value. Use for: (1) thread-context (user ID, request ID), (2) avoiding method parameters, (3) thread-unsafe objects (DateFormat, SimpleDateFormat), (4) performance optimization (thread-local buffers). ThreadLocal stores values in Thread object, retrieved via unique key. Thread A's get() returns Thread A's value even if multiple threads access same ThreadLocal. Dangers: memory leaks if not removed (value held until thread dies or explicitly removed), thread pools reuse threads (leftover values), overhead (hash lookup per access). In servlet containers, use try-finally or try-with-resources to clean up. Better alternatives: pass context through method parameters, or use Spring's RequestContext which auto-cleans. ThreadLocal is useful in frameworks (logging context, transaction context) but application code should prefer explicit parameters or dependency injection.
405.diagram: ThreadLocal: [Each thread has own value] → [Values isolated] → [Must clear on cleanup] → [Memory leak risk if ignored]

406.question: BlockingQueue and producer-consumer pattern
406.answer: BlockingQueue adds blocking operations to Queue: put (blocks if full), take (blocks if empty). Implements producer-consumer pattern directly—producers add items, consumers remove, queue handles synchronization and waiting. Common implementations: LinkedBlockingQueue (unbounded, efficient), ArrayBlockingQueue (bounded, fixed size), PriorityBlockingQueue (priority order), SynchronousQueue (handoff, no storage). put() blocks producer if queue full (backpressure), take() blocks consumer if empty. No need for manual synchronization—BlockingQueue handles it. Bounded queues are essential to prevent memory exhaustion under producer load. When producer faster than consumer, bounded queue forces producer to wait, protecting system from overload. SynchronousQueue for handoff (thread-to-thread), LinkedBlockingQueue for async decoupling.
406.diagram: Producer → BlockingQueue (bounded) → Consumer: [Full: producer blocks] [Empty: consumer blocks] (backpressure)

407.question: BlockingQueue implementation choices and trade-offs
407.answer: LinkedBlockingQueue: unbounded by default, reentrant lock, efficient, uses separate head/tail locks for concurrent put/take. ArrayBlockingQueue: bounded fixed-size, single lock (serializes put/take), worst under contention but memory-predictable. PriorityBlockingQueue: priority ordering, unbounded, slower than FIFO. SynchronousQueue: no storage, pure handoff, used in thread pools (newCachedThreadPool). DelayQueue: elements available only after delay, for scheduled tasks. For general producer-consumer: LinkedBlockingQueue (unbounded) or ArrayBlockingQueue (bounded). Bounded queues prevent unbounded memory growth; unbounded queues risk OutOfMemory under producer overload. Use bounded with proper rejection handler. Single-threaded producer/consumer: doesn't matter. Multi-threaded: LinkedBlockingQueue scales better due to separate locks. Never use unbounded queues without overflow handling—disk fills or OOM occurs.
407.diagram: LinkedBlockingQueue (separate locks, unbounded) > ArrayBlockingQueue (single lock, bounded) > SynchronousQueue (handoff)

408.question: Fail-fast vs fail-safe iterators and implications
408.answer: Fail-fast iterators (ArrayList, HashMap) throw ConcurrentModificationException if collection modified during iteration. Fast to detect, immediate failure. Fail-safe iterators (CopyOnWriteArrayList, ConcurrentHashMap) iterate over snapshot/copy, never throw exception even if modified. Collection modifications after iterator creation may or may not appear. Fail-fast is better for catching bugs (unintended concurrent modification). Fail-safe masks issues but is necessary for highly concurrent code. Never rely on fail-safe to hide lack of synchronization. In single-threaded code, fail-fast catches bugs. In concurrent code, use concurrent collections (ConcurrentHashMap) not fail-safe to hide problems. Iterator is not a snapshot in ConcurrentHashMap—it's weakly consistent (includes updates after creation, but not guaranteed).
408.diagram: Fail-fast (ArrayList: throws exception) vs Fail-safe (CopyOnWriteArrayList: ignores modifications, snapshot iteration)

409.question: Memory visibility in multi-threaded context and happens-before
409.answer: Visibility issue: Thread A writes to field x, Thread B reads x, but sees old value. Without synchronization, write cached in Thread A's CPU cache, not flushed to main memory. Thread B reads from its own cache, gets stale value. Happens-before relationships ensure visibility: if A happens-before B, A's effects visible to B. Synchronization creates happens-before edges: synchronized release happens-before synchronized acquire, volatile write happens-before volatile read, thread start happens-before thread code, thread code happens-before thread.join() return. Without happens-before, compiler can optimize away writes (if looks unused) or reorder operations. With proper synchronization, JVM inserts memory barriers ensuring visibility. Never assume visibility without synchronization. volatile for single-field visibility, synchronized for multi-field atomicity.
409.diagram: No synchronization: [Cache misses] → No visibility vs synchronized/volatile: [Happens-before] → [Memory barrier] → [Visible]

410.question: False sharing in CPU caches and prevention
410.answer: False sharing: two threads access different variables (x and y) that happen to be on same CPU cache line (typically 64 bytes). Thread A modifies x, invalidates entire cache line. Thread B's cache for y becomes invalid even though y unchanged. Thread B reloads from memory, causing unexpected stalls. In high-contention scenarios (millions of operations), false sharing tanks performance. Solutions: (1) pad variables with unused space to separate cache lines, (2) use LongAdder instead of AtomicLong (stripes updates), (3) @sun.misc.Contended (Java 8+, not public API), (4) use concurrent collections. Common in array access: if thread 0 updates array[0] and thread 1 updates array[1], same cache line causes false sharing. Avoid storing frequently-accessed per-thread data in array. Use ThreadLocal or LongAdder instead.
410.diagram: Cache line (64B): Thread0 writes x → invalidates entire line → Thread1's y becomes stale (false sharing)

411.question: What is a Java Future and CompletableFuture
411.answer: Future represents result of async computation—obtained after task submission, get() blocks until result ready. ExecutorService.submit(Callable) returns Future immediately. Future.get(timeout) blocks with timeout, Future.isDone() checks if completed, Future.cancel() attempts cancellation. Limitations: no composition (chaining), get() blocks, exceptions hidden until get(), callback-based programming difficult. CompletableFuture is a subclass adding callbacks/composition: thenApply (transform result), thenAccept (consume result), thenCompose (chain futures), handle (error handling). CompletableFuture.supplyAsync() runs task async and returns immediately. Callbacks executed when future completes, no blocking. Enables non-blocking pipeline. Use Future for simple submit-then-get patterns. Use CompletableFuture for chained async operations, error handling, or combining multiple futures.

412.question: ForkJoinPool and work-stealing algorithm
412.answer: ForkJoinPool is designed for divide-and-conquer tasks: break problem into smaller subproblems (fork), solve recursively, combine results (join). Used by parallel streams. Work-stealing: each thread has its own task queue (deque—double-ended queue). Thread works on its queue, when empty, steals tasks from other threads' tails. Avoids contention—stealing from tail (opposite end from thread's push) rarely conflicts. Optimal for recursive parallelism. Not suitable for I/O-bound tasks (threads spinning stealing instead of waiting). ForkJoinTask.fork() adds to own queue, join() waits for result. RecursiveTask for tasks with results, RecursiveAction for void tasks. Threshold: recurse while problem > threshold, sequential solve below. Thread count = availableProcessors, non-configurable.
412.diagram: ForkJoinPool: [Divide problem] → [fork() to queue] → [join() wait] → [Idle threads steal tasks] (work-stealing)

413.question: Volatile keyword and memory visibility guarantees
413.answer: volatile ensures visibility: writes visible immediately to other threads. Without volatile, writes cached in CPU register/L1 cache, not flushed to main memory. Another thread reads from cache, sees stale value. volatile forces writes to main memory and reads from main memory, bypassing caches. Provides happens-before relationship: volatile write happens-before subsequent volatile reads. Does NOT provide atomicity: x++ is not atomic even if x is volatile (race between read and write). volatile is lighter-weight than synchronized—no blocking, but only for single variables. Use for: (1) flags (boolean shutdown), (2) simple state (volatile reference to immutable object), (3) condition variables. Not for: compound operations, multiple-variable consistency, or I/O synchronization.
413.diagram: volatile: [Guarantees visibility] → [No atomicity] → Use for flags, not operations

414.question: Reentrant locks and when fairness matters
414.answer: Reentrant: same thread can acquire lock multiple times (reference counting). synchronized is reentrant by default. ReentrantLock explicitly supports this. Without reentrancy, a thread calling lock → calling another method that locks same lock = deadlock. Fairness: fair lock grants access in FIFO order, preventing starvation but slower. Non-fair lock: any waiting thread can acquire (JVM scheduler decides), faster but risk of starvation (same thread keeps getting lock). Fairness matters when: (1) latency is unpredictable (some requests starved forever), (2) system is overloaded (fair prevents some from progressing), (3) interactive systems (unpredictable lag). Cost: fair locks maintain ordered queue, more overhead. Use non-fair by default (better throughput), switch to fair if starvation detected. In most scenarios, fair vs non-fair difference is small unless extreme contention.
414.diagram: Reentrant (same thread can reacquire) vs Non-reentrant (deadlock risk); Fair (FIFO, slower) vs Non-fair (faster, starvation risk)

415.question: CountDownLatch and CyclicBarrier for thread coordination
415.answer: CountDownLatch: one or more threads wait for N events. count down starts at N, decrements toward 0. await() blocks until count reaches 0. One-time use—can't reset. Use for: (1) main thread waits for N worker threads to finish, (2) N threads wait for signal to start (count=1, all call await, one counts down). CyclicBarrier: N threads wait for each other at barrier point. Each thread calls await(), all blocked until all N arrive. Cyclic—reusable, resets after all threads pass. Use for: (1) parallel iterations (process batch, sync, process next batch), (2) staged computation. CountDownLatch for one-time synchronization. CyclicBarrier for repeated barriers. Phaser (Java 7+) is more flexible but complex. In practice: CountDownLatch for startup (wait for initialization), CyclicBarrier for synchronized phases.
415.diagram: CountDownLatch (one-time, one-way sync) vs CyclicBarrier (reusable, mutual sync at point)

416.question: Handling partial failures in distributed systems with retries and timeouts
416.answer: Partial failure: service A calls service B, B times out or fails, A must handle gracefully. Strategies: (1) Timeout—give up after duration (prevent indefinite blocking), (2) Retry—exponential backoff (avoid overwhelming failing service), (3) Circuit breaker—after N failures, fail fast without calling (detect cascading failure), (4) Bulkhead—limit thread pool size per dependency (isolate failures). Combined approach: call with timeout, retry with backoff on timeout/5xx, circuit breaker detects repeated failure pattern. Exponential backoff: 100ms, 200ms, 400ms, 800ms prevents thundering herd. Jitter (randomness) prevents synchronized retries from multiple clients. Circuit breaker: Closed (normal), Open (failing, fail fast), Half-open (testing recovery). Hystrix, Resilience4j implement these patterns. Essential for microservices stability.
416.diagram: Request → [Timeout] → Fail/Retry → [Backoff: exponential] → [Circuit Breaker: detect cascading] → [Bulkhead: isolate] → [Fallback]
